Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time during the week, but it never really stuck to a schedule. I'm the kind of person who learns by doing, so I'd often just dive into practice problems or hands-on stuff whenever I had a chunk of free time. With nine courses going on, it was hard to keep everything organized, so I'd just tackle whatever felt most urgent at the moment.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have weeks where I barely touched certain courses, then suddenly cram when deadlines approached. Being 23 and dealing with limited resources, I sometimes had to prioritize work or other responsibilities over studying. The regularity just wasn't there, even though I knew it probably should be.
Q3: Usually a notification about a deadline or seeing a message from a peer would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd just feel this wave of anxiety about falling behind and that would push me to check what I was missing. The peer support group I connected with also prompted me to log in more often since we'd coordinate through the platform sometimes.
Q4: Yeah, after a while logging in became almost automatic, especially in the mornings with coffee. The peer interactions helped make it feel less like a chore and more like checking in with people. Once I started engaging in discussions more regularly, it just became part of my daily routine. I'd say the social aspect really anchored that habit for me.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could pause and replay things while I practiced alongside them. Discussion forums were surprisingly useful too since my peers often explained concepts in ways that clicked better for me. I learn best when I can actually do something with my hands or work through problems, so any interactive content was gold.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just felt too passive for how I learn, and honestly, with limited time and resources, I had to pick my battles. Some of the optional quizzes I'd skip too unless I was really struggling with a topic. It wasn't that they weren't valuable, I just couldn't engage with everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to what I'd be tested on. Anything hands-on or interactive grabbed my attention way more than just reading. If my peers were also doing it or discussing it, that added value too because I could compare notes and learn from their approaches.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required ones had this pressure attached that forced engagement even when I wasn't feeling it. With optional stuff, my anxiety would sometimes kick in making me feel like I should do them, but then I'd talk myself out of it because something else was more pressing. It was this constant internal negotiation.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of materials and worked through them offline, especially when my internet was spotty or I needed to save data. I'd print things out when I could afford to and annotate them heavily. I also used YouTube tutorials and free resources that weren't part of the official course materials but helped me understand concepts better.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or practicing skills hands-on without any LMS activity showing. I'd study with peers in person, talking through concepts for hours, and none of that would register anywhere. Some of my best learning happened away from the screen, just me working through things physically.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then spend days working through it offline. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed and then disconnect. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that material ten times on my own device afterward.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd almost freeze, feeling overwhelmed by how much there was to do. During those times, I'd withdraw a bit, attend less, and just try to survive. The peer support helped pull me back, surprisingly.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed about money and other life stuff, the LMS felt like this mountain I couldn't climb. I'd log in with good intentions but end up just scrolling without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities made it hard to give the course my full attention, even when I wanted to.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't really there mentally. My anxiety made it hard to focus even when I was technically present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. My resilience isn't great, so when something felt too hard, I'd avoid it until the pressure became unbearable. It's a terrible cycle but I kept falling into it throughout the course.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd be relatively quiet until a deadline approached, then suddenly become super active. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and get me moving. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift completely.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me. Positive feedback would boost my motivation and make me want to engage more. But critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse, and I'd avoid that course for a while. The peer support helped me process feedback better since we'd discuss it together and normalize the experience.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules gave me a roadmap, but the sheer volume across nine courses was overwhelming. When things were broken into smaller, manageable pieces, I engaged more. When it felt like one massive wall of content, I'd shut down a bit.
Q19: Interactive elements and peer discussion options made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when content was purely text-based or when deadlines clustered together. The flexibility to access things at my own pace helped, but sometimes too much flexibility meant I'd drift and lose track.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week, honestly. You're logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos but not submitting because the anxiety of turning in imperfect work is paralyzing. Or you're preparing and learning but the actual assignment feels like too much to tackle. Activity doesn't always equal output.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was really high even though my attendance and assignment completion weren't great. I was learning effectively offline and through peer discussions, so the LMS didn't capture all my engagement. Sometimes quality of study matters more than quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns, like when you're active and what you're clicking on. But they miss the depth of engagement, the offline work, the peer conversations, the mental state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone's truly learning or just going through motions. They capture behavior, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, practicing, and connecting with peers about the content. It's a mental state, not just a behavior.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to get through anxious moments, or how much learning happened in offline conversations and hands-on practice. It wouldn't capture my internal struggle between wanting to do well and feeling paralyzed by anxiety. The human side of my engagement is completely invisible in the data.
